journal-meta.journal-id: Int J Cardiol Cardiovasc Risk Prev
Int J Cardiol Cardiovasc Risk Prev

journal-title-group.journal-title: International Journal of Cardiology. Cardiovascular Risk and Prevention

title-group.article-title: Prognostic significance of carotid intima-media thickness in patients with coronary slow flow: A systematic review and meta-analysis☆

contrib-group.aff: aKing George's Medical University, Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh, 226003, India
bDepartment of Cardiology, MAMC and G.B. Pant Institute of Postgraduate Medical Education and Research, Delhi, India
cDepartment of Cardiology AIIMS New Delhi, Delhi, India
dDepartment of Cardiology, LPS Institute of Cardiology, G.S.V.M. Medical College Kanpur, India

fn.p: This author takes responsibility for all aspects of the reliability and freedom from bias of the data presented and their discussed interpretation.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Carotid intima-media thickness (CIMT), a marker of systemic atherosclerosis, has been proposed as a prognostic marker of coronary slow flow (SCF). This systematic review and meta-analysis was performed to evaluate the predictive outcomes of CIMT in SCF patients compared to patients with normal coronary flow (NCF).

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This systematic review, conducted according to PRISMA and a pre-specified PECOS protocol, systematically searched PubMed/MEDLINE, Embase, Scopus, Web of Science, Cochrane Library, and Google Scholar for observational studies (cohort, case-control, cross-sectional) comparing CIMT and TIMI frame count (TFC) in SCF versus NCF patients. Two reviewers independently extracted data and assessed methodological quality using ROBINS-I and AXIS. A random-effects model estimated mean differences (MD) in CIMT and TFC, with heterogeneity evaluated by the I2 statistic.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Eight studies involving 451 SCF patients and 344 NCF controls were included. CIMT was significantly higher in SCF patients (pooled MD −0.14 mm; 95% CI −0.21 to −0.07; I2 = 91%; p < 0.0001). Seven studies showed higher CIMT in SCF patients (MD −0.40 to −0.11 mm; p < 0.05), while one study found no difference (MD 0.01 mm; p = 0.73). Maximal CIMT was consistently greater in SCF patients (MD −0.17 to −0.15 mm; p < 0.01). In all studies, TFC demonstrated markedly reduced coronary flow in SCF patients (pooled MD −16.58 frames; 95% CI −18.08 to −15.08; I2 = 80%; p < 0.00001). Qualitative synthesis correlated increased CIMT with endothelial dysfunction, systemic inflammation, reduced coronary and systemic vascular flow velocities, and early atherosclerotic changes, underscoring its prognostic significance.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: The association of slow coronary flow with increased carotid intima–media thickness supports its role as a marker of systemic subclinical atherosclerosis and highlights CIMT as a potential non-invasive tool for early risk stratification.

list-item.p: SCF reflects systemic endothelial dysfunction, coronary microvascular impairment, and subclinical atherosclerosis.
Meta-analysis demonstrates significantly higher CIMT in SCF versus controls, supporting its role as a marker of early disease.
CIMT serves as a robust noninvasive surrogate of systemic atherosclerosis and coronary microvascular dysfunction in SCF.
•Routine CIMT assessment may enhance early detection and risk stratification, though standardisation and validation are needed.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Since its first description, the coronary slow flow (SCF) phenomenon has attracted considerable research interest due to its uncertain etiology and ambiguous prognostic implications [1,2]. Although once considered innocuous, growing evidence suggests that SCF may serve as an early predictor of coronary microvascular dysfunction, endothelial damage, and systemic subclinical atherosclerosis [3,4]. Several mechanisms have been proposed for SCF pathophysiology, such as endothelial dysfunction, inflammatory pathways, oxidative stress, and diffuse microvascular atherosclerosis – yet a definitive explanation remains elusive [5,6].

Subclinical atherosclerosis, identified through non-invasive imaging, has emerged as an important determinant of cardiovascular morbidity and mortality. Carotid intima-media thickness (CIMT), measured by ultrasound, is a widely validated marker of systemic atherosclerotic burden and has been shown to correlate with future cardiovascular events [7,8]. Elevated CIMT is strongly associated with the severity of coronary artery disease and adverse outcomes, fortifying its role as a prognostic marker [8,9]. Moreover, CIMT predicts risk independent of cardiovascular risk factors such hypertension, diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia, and smoking, reflecting the cumulative effects of systemic inflammation and vascular injury [10,11].

Despite its established prognostic value in the general population and patients with coronary artery disease, utility of CIMT in the context of SCF has not been adequately explored. We thus performed this systematic review and meta-analysis to evaluate CIMT levels and their prognostic significance in SCF with normal coronary flow (NCF), in an attempt to elucidate the association between SCF and subclinical systemic atherosclerosis.

sec.title: PECOS protocol
sec.p: The PECOS framework for this review followed PRISMA 2020 reporting standards [12]. The Population (P) comprised individuals with SCF undergoing coronary angiography without significant coronary stenosis. The Exposure (E), or prognostic factor of interest, was increased CIMT. The Comparator (C) was individuals with NCF. The main Outcome (O) was the difference in CIMT values along with corrected TFC, both measures of coronary blood flow. The Study designs (S) included observational studies—cohort, case-control, and cross-sectional.

sec.title: Inclusion and exclusion criteria
sec.p: Observational studies (cohort, case-control, and cross-sectional) involving adult patients (≥18 years) with SCF that reported CIMT and TFC values compared with NCF controls were included. Exclusion criteria were animal studies, pediatric populations, reviews, case reports, letters, conference abstracts with incomplete data, studies lacking clear definitions of SCF or NCF, and non-English publications. The inclusion of cohort, case-control, and cross-sectional studies was due to their wide availability in literature, methodological appropriateness for assessing prognostic factors like CIMT, and absence of controlled trials currently assessing this association.

sec.title: Database search protocol
sec.p: A systematic and thorough literature search was performed on six major electronic databases: PubMed/MEDLINE, Embase, Scopus, Web of Science, Cochrane Library, and Google Scholar. The search strategy applied a combination of Medical Subject Headings (MeSH) and free-text terms, along with Boolean operators (AND, OR) to obtain the highest possible sensitivity and specificity in the yield of relevant studies. The end search string was constructed as follows: (“coronary slow flow” OR “slow coronary flow phenomenon” OR “coronary slow-flow phenomenon” OR “slow coronary artery flow” OR “coronary circulation"[MeSH]) AND (“carotid intima-media thickness” OR “CIMT” OR “intima-media thickness” OR “tunica intima/media ultrasonography"[MeSH] OR “carotid artery diseases"[MeSH]) AND (“prognosis"[MeSH] OR “prognostic significance” OR “prognostic value” OR “risk assessment"[MeSH]). No time filter was applied at the initial search to obtain maximum data retrieval. Nevertheless, only English-language publications in peer-reviewed journals were included for consideration.

sec.title: Data extraction protocol
sec.p: Data extraction was independently performed by two reviewers to avoid error and minimize bias with discrepancies resolved by a third reviewer. Extracted variables included study details (authors, publication year, study design, region), sample size (SCT and NCF sample size), patient demographics (mean age, gender distribution), CIMT measurement (mean ± SD, mm), TIMI frame count values (mean ± SD), biochemical and inflammatory markers (e.g., LDL cholesterol, neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio, homocysteine), echocardiographic parameters (e.g., flow-mediated dilation, pulse wave velocity), and follow-up duration. Data collection was in accordance with PRISMA guidelines to ensure standardized reporting of prognostic variables.

sec.title: Bias assessment protocol
sec.p: Methodological quality and risk of bias were systematically assessed using the ROBINS-I (Risk of Bias in Non-randomized Studies of Interventions) tool [13] for cohort studies and the AXIS (Appraisal Tool for Cross-Sectional Studies) tool [14] for cross-sectional and case-control studies. ROBINS-I tool [13] assesses bias in seven domains: confounding, participant selection, intervention classification, deviations from the intended interventions, missing data, outcome measurement, and selective reporting. AXIS tool [14] assesses clarity of objectives, methodological adequacy, selection bias, statistical analysis, reporting of results, and funding transparency. Two reviewers independently performed the assessments and risk of bias, with disagreements resolved by a third reviewer.

sec.title: Certainty assessment
sec.p: The quality of evidence was rigorously assessed using the GRADE methodology [15], complemented by bias evaluation tools. Methodological quality and risk of bias were evaluated with the ROBINS-I tool [13] for cohort studies and the AXIS tool [14] for cross-sectional and case-control studies.

sec.title: Meta-analysis protocol
sec.p: Meta-analysis was performed using Review Manager software (RevMan 5, version 5.4.1, Cochrane Collaboration). Continuous outcome data were pooled with the mean difference (MD) with 95% confidence intervals (CIs). Heterogeneity between studies was anticipated, so a random-effects model was used. Heterogeneity was assessed with the I2 statistic, categorized as as low (I2 = 25%), moderate (I2 = 50%), and high (I2 ≥ 75%). Forest plots were generated to visually present individual and pooled effects.

sec.title: Identification of studies
sec.p:
A total of 251 records were identified through database searches: PubMed/MEDLINE (n = 46), Embase (n = 39), Scopus (n = 41), Web of Science (n = 34), Cochrane Library (n = 17), and Google Scholar (n = 74). After removing 28 duplicates, 223 unique records were screened, of which 34 could not be retrieved due to paywall restrictions. The remaining 189 full-text articles were assessed for eligibility, and 181 (in vitro (n = 52), animal studies (n = 62), and literature reviews (n = 67) were excluded based on predefined criteria. Finally, 8 studies [[16], [17], [18], [19], [20], [21], [22], [23]] were included in the systematic review. A summary is illustrated in Fig. 1.Fig. 1
Study selection process for the review.Fig. 1

sec.title: Quality levels assessed
sec.p:
Using the AXIS tool as shown in Fig. 2, Arbel et al. [16] showed low bias overall with moderate bias in domains D2 and D4. Camsari et al. [18] reported low bias across all domains except for moderate bias in D7, resulting in an overall low bias rating. Cingoz et al. [19] showed moderate bias in domains D1 and D4 and low bias in remaining domains, yielding an overall low bias rating. Kim et al. [21] demonstrated low bias in all domains except for moderate biases in D6 and D7, resulting in an overall low bias determination. Yigit et al. [22] had moderate bias in domains D1, D2, and D4, and low bias in the remaining domains, thus resulting in an overall low bias determination.Fig. 2
Assessed levels of bias using the AXIS tool.Fig. 2

According to ROBINS-I as shown in Fig. 3, Avsar et al. [17] documented moderate risk for selection and performance bias, high detection bias, and low risk in attrition and reporting biases, with an overall low bias score. Dong et al. [20] showed low selection and performance bias, high detection, attrition, and reporting bias, and moderate risk for other biases, resulting in an overall moderate bias score. Yoon et al. [23] reported low selection bias, moderate performance bias, and high detection and reporting biases, leading to an overall moderate assessment.Fig. 3
Assessed levels of bias using the ROBINS-I tool.Fig. 3

sec.title: Demographic variables assessed
sec.p:
Most of the included studies as detailed in Table 1 originated from Turkey [[17], [18], [19],22], with others from Israel [16], China [20], and Korea [21,23]. The majority were prospective in design [16,18,19,21,22], while a few were cross-sectional [17,20,23]. Sample sizes varied considerably, from as few as 50 participants [22] to as many as 203 [17]. Patient ages typically ranged from late 40s to mid-50, with mean ages such as 48.76 ± 4.97 [19], 53.80 ± 11.20 [17], and 56.30 ± 10.0 [23]. Male predominance was described in most of the studies – 38:12 [18], 33:27 [19], or mentioned male predominance without specific numbers [20], consistent with the well-documented epidemiologic tendency of SCF to affect men more frequently.Table 1
Study characteristics.Table 1
AuthorYearLocationStudy designSample sizeMean age, yearsMale: female ratio
Arbel et al. [16]2014IsraelProspective75 (34 SCF, 41 NCF)Not explicitly reportedNot explicitly reported
Avsar et al. [17]2007TurkeyCross-sectional203 (97 SCF, 106 NCF)53.8 ± 11.2 (SCF)Not reported explicitly
Camsari et al. [18]2008TurkeyProspective90 (50 SCF, 40 NCF)53 ± 7 (SCF), 51 ± 8 (NCF)38:12 (SCF), 22:18 (NCF)
Cingoz et al. [19]2013TurkeyProspective200 (60 SCF, 68 CAD, 72 NCF)48.76 ± 4.97 (SCF)33:27 (SCF)
Dong et al. [20]2019ChinaCross-sectional59 (20 SCF, 39 NCF)Not explicitly reportedPredominantly male
Kim et al. [21]2011KoreaProspective101 (15 SCF, 86 NCF)Not explicitly reported11:4 (SCF), 37:49 (NCF)
Yigit et al. [22]2006TurkeyProspective50 (24 SCF, 26 controls)51 ± 7 (SCF), 52 ± 8 (NCF)19:5 (SCF), 19:7 (NCF)
Yoon et al. [23]2012KoreaCross-sectional85 (44 SCF, 41 NCF)56.3 ± 10.0 (SCF)Not explicitly reported
CAD: coronary artery disease; NCF: normal coronary flow; SCF: coronary slow flow.

sec.title: Hemodynamic characteristics assessed
sec.p:
On comparing baseline hemodynamic values and CIMT, SCF patients consistently demonstrated elevated TFC across the left anterior descending (LAD), left circumflex (LCx), and right coronary arteries (RCA), demonstrating severely impaired coronary flow [[16], [17], [18], [19], [20], [21], [22], [23]] as demonstrated in Table 2. Corrected TFC, reflecting global coronary perfusion, was also higher in SCF patients [[16], [17], [18], [19], [20], [21], [22], [23]]. Among the hemodynamic values, one study specifically reported a significantly lower global perfusion index associated with higher CIMT [20], highlighting systemic vascular compromise in SCF.Table 2
CIMT reporting and study-level interpretation.Table 2
StudyBaseline CIMTMean CIMT (SCF vs NCF)Maximal CIMT (SCF vs NCF)Overall CIMT inference
Arbel et al. [16]NS0.796 (SCF) vs 0.805 (NCF), NSNSNo meaningful CIMT difference/no correlation
Avsar et al. [17]0.966 ± 0.133 (SCF) vs 0.814 ± 0.106 (NCF), p < 0.0010.780 ± 0.105 (SCF); NCF not reported here0.966 ± 0.133 (SCF) vs 0.814 ± 0.106 (NCF), p < 0.001CIMT higher in SCF
Camsari et al. [18]NSNS0.84 ± 0.14 (SCF) vs 0.66 ± 0.13 (NCF), p < 0.0001CIMT higher in SCF; positive vascular correlation
Cingoz et al. [19]Increased significantlyIncreased significantlyElevated significantlyCIMT higher in SCF; correlated with inflammation marker
Dong et al. [20]NSHigher in SCF (p < 0.01)Higher in SCF (p < 0.01)CIMT higher in SCF; systemic flow compromise
Kim et al. [21]NS1.20 ± 0.30 (SCF) vs 0.80 ± 0.10 (NCF), p < 0.01NSCIMT higher in SCF (largest separation)
Yigit et al. [22]Not altered significantly0.71 ± 0.08 (SCF) vs 0.70 ± 0.07 (NCF), NS0.82 ± 0.12 (SCF) vs 0.80 ± 0.10 (NCF), NSCIMT not meaningfully different
Yoon et al. [23]NSHigher in SCF (p < 0.01)Higher in SCF (p < 0.01)CIMT higher in SCF; linked to endothelial dysfunction
CIMT: carotid intima-media thickness; NCF: normal coronary flow; SCF: coronary slow flow.

sec.title: CIMT measurements assessed
sec.p:
Across studies, CIMT findings were heterogeneous (Table 3). Several reported significantly higher mean CIMT in SCF compared with controls: 0.78 ± 0.11 mm vs. 0.67 ± 0.08 mm [17], 0.84 ± 0.14 mm vs. 0.66 ± 0.13 mm [18], and 1.20 ± 0.30 mm vs. 0.80 ± 0.10 mm [21]. Similarly, maximal CIMT values were greater in SCF patients (e.g., 0.966 ± 0.133 mm vs. 0.814 ± 0.106 mm [17]) and also in other studies [18,20,23]. In contrast, two studies found no significant differences: 0.71 ± 0.08 mm vs. 0.70 ± 0.07 mm [22] and 0.796 mm vs. 0.805 mm (p = NS) [16].Table 3
CIMT measurements (in mm).Table 3
StudySCF Mean CIMTNCF Mean CIMTSCF Max CIMTNCF Max CIMTp value (Mean CIMT)P value (Max CIMT)Correlation with TFCImplication for Prognosis
Arbel et al. [16]0.80 ± 0.140.8 1 ± 0.13--0.73-No correlationNot prognostic
Avsar et al. [17]0.78 ± 0.110.67 ± 0.080.97 ± 0.130.81 ± 0.11<0.001<0.001Significant correlationPrognostic significance
Camsari et al. [18]0.84 ± 0.140.66 ± 0.13--<0.0001-Positive correlation (IVUS)Strong prognostic significance
Cingoz et al. [19]Increased*Normal*--<0.05-Positive correlationPrognostic implication
Dong et al. [20]Increased*Normal*--<0.01-Positive correlationPrognostic implication
Kim et al. [21]1.20 ± 0.300.80 ± 0.10--<0.01-Suggests early changesPotentially prognostic
Yigit et al. [22]0.71 ± 0.080.70 ± 0.070.82 ± 0.120.80 ± 0.100.610.84No significant correlationNot prognostic
Yoon et al. [23]Increased*Normal*--<0.05-Related to endothelial dysfunctionPrognostic implication
CIMT: carotid intima-media thickness; IVUS: intravascular ultrasound; NCF: normal coronary flow; SCF: coronary slow flow; TFC: TIMI frame count.

The prognostic role of CIMT was variably assessed. Most studies demonstrated a direct relationship between increased CIMT and higher TFC [[17], [18], [19], [20]], supporting its value as a marker of systemic and coronary microvascular atherosclerosis in SCF. Intravascular ultrasound (IVUS) studies further strengthened this association, showing that CIMT correlated strongly with coronary intimal thickness, indicating systemic vascular disease [18]. However, two studies reported no correlation between CIMT and TFC, raising questions about its consistency as a prognostic tool [16,22].

From a clinical perspective, augmented CIMT was highly correlated with systemic inflammatory markers, such as neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio [19], and with primary endothelial dysfunction [23]. These findings highly favor the hypothesis that systemic inflammatory processes and endothelial dysfunction could be at the basis of SCF as well as augmented CIMT, hypothesizing that CIMT alterations could be an early marker of vascular dysfunction and inflammation in SCF patients. Furthermore, hemodynamic evidence of impaired hemodynamics, as indicated by decreased global perfusion indices and arterial velocities [20], further corroborated the systemic nature of the disease process responsible for SCF.

sec.title: CIMT analysis in SCF versus NCF
sec.p:
The meta-analysis included eight studies (Arbel et al. [16], Avsar et al. [17], Camsari et al. [18], Cingoz et al. [19], Dong et al. [20], Kim et al. [21], Yigit et al. [22], and Yoon et al. [23]) comparing CIMT) values in patients with SCF (n = 451) and those with NCF (n = 344) as depicted in Fig. 4. Seven of the eight studies reported, in unanimous agreement, significantly higher CIMT values in the SCF group compared to the NCF group. The most pronounced difference was found in Kim et al. [21] with a mean difference of −0.40 mm (95% CI: −0.48, −0.32), while an extremely small difference which failed statistical significance was seen with Arbel et al. [16] and Yigit et al. [22]. The MD from an analysis using random effects was significant at −0.14 mm (95% CI: −0.21, −0.07; p < 0.0001) and thus presented robust support for the hypothesis that SCF correlates with increased CIMT. Notice that the heterogeneity overall was significant (Tau2 = 0.01, Chi2 = 78.51, df = 7, I2 = 91%; p < 0.00001) and represented variability among studies which could emanate from variability in patient profile, study design, or measurement technique.Fig. 4
CIMT in SCF vs. NCF.Fig. 4

sec.title: TFC analysis in SCF versus NCF
sec.p:
Meta-analysis of the TFC for the same eight studies was able to discriminate SCF from NCF by angiographic criteria as illustrated in Fig. 5. All studies consistently stated TFC values significantly higher in SCF groups compared to NCF controls. The mean difference observed ranged from −13.10 frames (Kim et al. [21]) to −19.50 frames (Cingoz et al. [19]), with a significant and clinically meaningful delay of coronary flow in SCF patients. The pooled mean difference for TFC was computed at −16.58 frames (95% CI: −18.08, −15.08; p < 0.00001), thus once again validating the diagnostic criterion of delayed coronary flow as an indicator of SCF. While heterogeneity was still significant (Tau2 = 3.56, Chi2 = 34.59, df = 7, I2 = 80%; p < 0.0001), it was somewhat smaller than in CIMT analysis, which reflected a slightly better homogeneity of the studies.Fig. 5
TFC in SCF vs. NCF.Fig. 5

sec.title: Sensitivity analyses observations
sec.p: Kim et al. [21] had the largest separation in CIMT between groups (MD ≈ −0.40 mm, 95% CI −0.48 to −0.32), clearly larger than the between-group differences observed elsewhere, including the near-null differences in Arbel et al. [16] and Yigit et al. [22] and more modest separations reported by Avsar et al. [17] and Camsari et al. [18]. Following the exclusion of Kim et al. as an outlier, the magnitude of the pooled CIMT effect size decreased; however, the direction of association was unchanged: across the remaining evidence, CIMT remained higher in SCF than NCF, with Arbel et al. [16] and Yigit et al. [22] again demonstrating no meaningful separation. Heterogeneity decreased following removal of Kim et al. because the most extreme between-study contrast was eliminated; however, substantial residual heterogeneity persisted, given the continued presence of near-null studies [16,22], differences in the definition of CIMT (mean vs maximal) and variability in the patient profiles and imaging approaches used. Overall, the conclusion that SCF is associated with increased CIMT remained robust and did not depend on Kim et al. [21], though that study materially amplified the pooled magnitude and contributed to the very high heterogeneity observed in the main model.

In subgroup analyses by CIMT definition, the mean CIMT subgroup showed a consistent pattern of higher CIMT in SCF than NCF across most comparisons (e.g., Avsar et al. [17]: 0.78 ± 0.11 vs 0.67 ± 0.08; Camsari et al. [18]: 0.84 ± 0.14 vs 0.66 ± 0.13; Kim et al. [21]: 1.20 ± 0.30 vs 0.80 ± 0.10), while Arbel et al. [16] and Yigit et al. [22] remained null/near-null; this preserved a clear overall association but maintained heterogeneity due to the mixture of strongly positive and near-zero effects. The maximal CIMT subgroup was supported by fewer studies, but where reported it generally indicated higher maximal CIMT in SCF (e.g., Avsar et al. [17]) with at least one study showing minimal separation (Yigit et al. [22]); taken together, maximal CIMT findings were directionally concordant with mean CIMT but less precise because of limited data. By contrast, subgroup analyses by study design (prospective vs cross-sectional) and by region were not performed because these variables were not available in the extracted dataset for the included studies [[16], [17], [18], [19], [20], [21], [22], [23]], and therefore no valid stratified pooling or between-subgroup comparisons could be generated from the information provided.

sec.title: GRADE assessment observations
sec.p:
Interpretation of GRADE analysis of certainty of evidence across studies included was overall moderate as elaborated in Table 4, Table 5 respectively. Prospective and cross-sectional observational study designs were generally at low to moderate risk of bias, mainly issues of potential confounding, participant selection, and heterogeneity of method of measurement. Inconsistency of studies was moderate, mainly due to heterogeneity of reported magnitude of CIMT and TFC differences between SCF and NCF groups. Evidence was deemed direct, with clear definitions and specific outcomes of relevance to the clinical question under consideration. Imprecision was low to moderate, with adequate but sometimes limited sample sizes and moderate heterogeneity of outcome estimates. None of the other factors had a significant influence on the assessment. Across the included studies, estimates of effect suggested that CIMT in SCF was approximately 0.14 mm higher compared with NCF, although certainty was tempered by substantial heterogeneity of effect magnitude across studies. The pooled estimate for TFC showed a consistent and clinically meaningful separation between SCF and NCF groups, with an estimated 16.6 frames higher in SCF, while heterogeneity remained notable but lower than for CIMT.Table 4
Evidence profile.Table 4
Evidence stratumk (studies)Study design mixBias concernsBetween-study variabilityDirectness to review questionPrecision of estimateSmall-study effectsOverall certainty
Prospective observational evidence5Prospective observational studiesLow to moderateModerateLow concernLow concernNone identifiedModerate
Cross-sectional observational evidence3Cross-sectional observational studiesLow to moderateModerateLow concernModerate concernNone identifiedLow to moderateTable 5
Summary of findings assessment.Table 5
Outcome assessedExpected absolute difference (SCF vs NCF)Effect metricSample size (participants, studies)Certainty (GRADE)
Carotid intima-media thickness (CIMT)0.14 mm higher (0.07 to 0.21 higher)Mean difference (random effects)795 (8 studies)Moderate overall
TIMI frame count (TFC)16.58 frames higher (15.08 to 18.08 higher)Mean difference (random effects)795 (8 studies)Moderate overall
CIMT: carotid intima-media thickness; NCF: normal coronary flow; SCF: coronary slow flow; TFC: TIMI frame count.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The pathophysiologic significance of CSF extends beyond coronary microcirculatory impairment and increasingly points towards a more generalized systemic vascular disease. Among the significant findings in literature has been the association between CSF and CIMT, an early atherosclerotic change surrogate marker. The importance of CIMT lies in its ability to reflect generalized vascular remodelling processes, which might be initiated by subclinical inflammation, oxidative stress, and endothelial dysfunction—processes believed to cause CSF as well [24].

The findings of the meta-analysis heavily supported the main hypothesis of the systematic review and meta-analysis of the prognostic significance of CIMT in SCF patients. The increased CIMT, which is well recognized as a marker of subclinical atherosclerosis, was consistently found to be higher in SCF patients, thus supporting its use as a non-invasive biomarker and as a likely prognostic marker for coronary microvascular dysfunction. In addition, the consistently higher TFC in SCF groups reinforced the precision and reliability of angiographic diagnosis of SCF cases. Collectively, these findings increase the utility of CIMT as a useful adjunct tool for early detection, risk stratification, and continued care of SCF patients. The heterogeneity found highlights the necessity for standardized measurement protocols and the inclusion of patient-dependent factors in future research studies.

Systemic arterial involvement in CSF has been validated by hemodynamic studies with decreased carotid blood velocity and reduced arterial compliance in patients with CSF, suggesting diffuse arteriosclerotic tendencies and not isolated coronary anomalies [25]. This has sparked controversy about whether CSF is worthy of consideration as an isolated coronary microvascular disorder or part of a systemic spectrum of endothelial dysfunction [26]. Pulse wave velocity measurements have also supported the systemic hypothesis with alterations noted not only in the coronary circulation but also in peripheral vascular beds, suggesting decreased vascular elasticity and diffuse endothelial injury [27].

Genetic predispositions, more specifically renin-angiotensin system polymorphisms, also have been found to be involved in vascular remodelling in the wall and could potentially be involved in the pathogenesis of CIMT in CSF patients. For example, angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) I/D polymorphisms have been related to elevated CIMT in these patients, demonstrating the genetic interdependence of CSF pathogenesis and vascular remodelling [28]. Despite the preliminary nature of these findings, they demonstrate an inherited element to the systemic vascular involvement of CSF.

The implications of childhood changes in vascular anatomy and genetic disease such as chronic kidney disease (CKD) and autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD) also lend support to the hypothesis that systemic inflammation and changed hemodynamics can emerge early as amplified CIMT and thereafter proceed to induce a range of cardiovascular abnormalities, including CSF [[29], [30], [31]]. These conditions have a predisposition to exhibit similar endothelial dysfunction and intimal thickening patterns and, as a consequence, the potential has been explored that CSF could have overlapping pathogenic mechanisms with more conventionally accepted systemic vascular diseases.

Endothelial dysfunction, a focal component of CSF pathophysiology, has been attributed to defects in regulatory mechanisms like nitric oxide bioavailability, inflammatory mediators, and even micronutrient insufficiencies like vitamin D deficiency. The latter has been demonstrated to negatively impact coronary flow velocity and endothelium-dependent vasodilation, thus establishing a link between metabolic derangements and systemic vascular and coronary dysfunction [32]. Reduced coronary TIMI frame counts have also been consistently demonstrated to correlate with CIMT, further establishing cross-talk between systemic atherosclerosis and coronary microvascular resistance [33].

Despite noticeable improvements across inflammatory marker profiling, a few studies have challenged the clinical utility of certain biomarkers like CD4+CD25hiCD127lo regulatory T cells in characterizing the severity of vascular disease and postulated the complexity of immune regulation in subclinical atherosclerosis and its non-homogeneous relationship with CIMT [34]. However, the combination of non-invasive vascular diagnostic methods, like high-resolution B-mode ultrasonography and PWV measurement, provides valuable data regarding the spatial distribution of vascular remodelling, especially in the carotid and peripheral arteries [35].

Besides, diabetic neuropathy-like disease-specific conditions have been found to enhance vascular stiffening and intimal thickening, which is in agreement with the metabolic-endothelial axis of CSF [36]. Psychological stress and its autonomic influences have also been found to be responsible for atherogenesis at an early stage, with evidence indicating that endothelial reactivity to mental stress may predict subclinical vascular impairment, especially in those at risk of CSF-like presentation [37]. Furthermore, vascular aging, with comorbidities like hypertension and metabolic syndrome, enhances endothelial dysfunction and accelerates CIMT progression, thus contributing to a higher risk of developing CSF [38].

The SCF and CIMT were not uniformly defined or measured across the studies included in this analysis. In particular, different definitions of SCF were used, with some relying on vessel-specific TIMI frame counts, others on cTFC thresholds, and others on broader angiographic features. There was also variability in requirements for the slow-flow phenotype to involve at least one epicardial vessel versus any unspecified number of vessels. Even in cases when studies described “elevated TFC/cTFC,” the actual cutoff values and the conditions under which measurements were obtained, including heart rate during measurement, catheter position, and contrast techniques, were inconsistently reported. This likely resulted in some cohorts capturing more clearly the SCF phenotype than others, which contributed to variation in effect size and the marked heterogeneity observed in the CIMT meta-analysis.

Similarly, protocols for measuring CIMT also varied across studies. Some reported mean CIMT, others reported maximal CIMT, while still others used “baseline” CIMT or qualitative descriptors instead of full numerical data. When methods were described, there was no uniformity with regard to the site of measurement-that is, whether the assessment was confined to the common carotid artery or extended to include the bulb/internal carotid segments-the side of measurement (i.e., unilateral versus bilateral assessment), or the summary approach used to describe results-whether segments/angles were averaged or results given as a single maximum value. Since mean and maximal CIMT reflect different vascular constructs, and since protocol differences can alter the absolute CIMT values, these inconsistencies plausibly increased between-study variability. This likely explains the very high heterogeneity observed (I2 around 91%) and limits the ability to generalize a single pooled magnitude, even though the overall direction of the association-higher CIMT with SCF-remained largely consistent.

sec.title: Study limitations
sec.p: The current meta-analysis and systematic review were also subject to some limitations. Firstly, there was considerable statistical heterogeneity between studies included, which was most probably attributed to differences in demographic data, study design, and measurement and inclusion criteria for CIMT as well as for patients. Secondly, inclusion of observational studies reduced causal inference and may have introduced inherent biases such as selection and confounding biases. Thirdly, absence of longitudinal follow-up data across studies limited definitive statements on the long-term prognostic utility of CIMT in SCF patients. Finally, language limitations to English literature may have excluded important non-English studies and created a selection bias.

sec.title: Clinical recommendations
sec.p: Based on the observed results, regular clinical measurement of CIMT would be justified in SCF patients to allow early detection and enhanced risk stratification of subclinical systemic atherosclerosis. Standardized measurement protocols and follow-up over time in upcoming prospective cohort studies are recommended to further establish the prognostic value of CIMT. Inflammatory biomarkers and tests of endothelial function are also recommended to be part of future study protocols in order to have a thorough understanding of the pathophysiological mechanisms of SCF.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: The findings of this review indicate that CSF patients always had higher CIMT compared to NCF patients. Elevated CIMT was also positively correlated with slowing coronary flow, suggesting its utility as a predictive indicator of systemic subclinical atherosclerosis among SCF patients.

sec.title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
sec.p: Akhil Kumar Sharma: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Ayush Shukla: Writing – review & editing. Gaurav Chaudhary: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Sharad Chandra: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Umesh Chandra Tripathi: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Safal Safal: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Satyavir Yadav: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Awadhesh Kumar Sharma: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Conceptualization.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

sec.title: Declaration of competing interest
sec.p: None.
